thread.title: Best BIFL Office Chair?
thread.body: I sit at my desk for 10 hours a day. Need something that won't break my back.
comments: Herman Miller Aeron. It's expensive but absolutely worth it. My first one lasted 15 years.
Honestly, the Steelcase Leap V2 is better if you are tall. I found the Aeron cuts off my circulation.
Just grab a cheap generic mesh chair from amazon, they are all the same anyway.

expected_mentions.flawOrCaveat: Aeron cuts off circulation if you are tall

thread.title: Kitchen Aid mixers...
thread.body: Are the new KitchenAid Artisan mixers actually BIFL or did they switch to plastic gears?
comments: The Artisan models are still great. The Professional 600 line is better though.
I bought a stand mixer last year and the gears stripped on the first batch of bread. Terrible value.
Mine is from 1990 and still purrs like a kitten.

expected_mentions.flawOrCaveat: gears stripped on the first batch

thread.title: [BIFL] Just got these in the mail from Darn Tough.
comments: I sent them four pairs of socks that I wore holes through. Less than a week later, four brand new pairs, no questions asked. They're the most comfortable socks I've ever worn, merino wool, and (despite my abuse), extremely durable. They also truly honor their unconditional lifetime guarantee. I can't recommend Darn Tough enough!
Oh, you bought socks.
They sent me four replacement pairs for ones I returned that I wore through. Sorry I didn't write a very good title. :(

I may or may not work at a consumer product testing company that may or may not have done testing for Darn Tough VT and the results may or may not have proven that these are the most legit socks FUCKING EVER.

thread.title: Dunno about life exactly but these leather Chucks have lasted 6 years and I wear them a few times a week.
comments: I love all-stars, especially with leather uppers. But I've never had a pair last longer than two years. The soles just aren't designed to last. As soon as they wear down 1/4" or so, everything starts to separate. Shoe glue buys me another couple months and then they're done.
Wear them where? To bed?!? They don't look like they've left the house!
Classic black leather sleep shoes.

expected_mentions.flawOrCaveat: soles aren't designed to last, everything starts to separate

thread.title: These Heuer vises last forever
thread.body:
Got mine 2 years ago, slammed it with a hammer quite a few times. Not even a scratch in the paint. The mounting points for the bolts are still unmarred too, despite having used an impact wrench to mount it in my previous garage. Weighs 12 kilos. Gonna reinstall this thing again!

My uncle has one for over 20 years and also looks almost as good, albeit a bit greasy.
Still runs smoothly.
comments: Old Machinist and welder here, I sense almost any vise lasts forever....
If you're lucky,  you'll destroy 3-4 benches underneath this treasure.

Well I used to have a random no-name brand vise from a store like harbor freight, it did last a few years, but the jaws eventual wore out.
So I decided to get a slightly bigger one.

It wasn’t completely FUBAR, so I gave it away to a friend who didn’t have one.
He patched up the jaws by grinding them down a bit and it’s now installed in his shed.
The jaws are consumables with heavy use, that's why you can usually replace them.

thread.title: Just managed to clean up this beautiful old Graef slicer.
thread.body: Got it for 10 bucks from a guy near me who probably wasn't invested enough to spend almost three hours, one roll of kitchen towel and a full bottle of spirit cleaner [ if it's called like that in english :D ] to remove the fat and grease residues of at least three generations. I forgot to take a before-cleaning pic for comparison but the cleanup wasn't fun, believe me! Upon closer inspection it isn't even fully clean yet, but I'd at least consider it food-safe again.
comments:
I use them pretty often. Not as bad as it seems. At least depending on what you use it for. 

I mainly use it for bread, Tofu, and some Seitan. ( I don't eat meat or cheese) The fresher you wipe it the better it is. Also cutting a slice of bread after using it for something greasy is amazing. Soaks up much of the grease on the blade and the rounded plate that the food touches after it's cut. 

Since I love to make my own deli slices from tofu or Seitan these things are a godsent. I'm in Germany so I eat tons of bread. It is fresh so much longer if you just cut a slice every time you need one.

And it will continue to be a pain in the ass to clean. Every. Single. Time.

Nice slicer though!
As much as I'd like to contradict you, I think you're right :,)
10 bucks? Amazing deal!
Are you going to feed the cat underneath the door Kramer?

expected_mentions.flawOrCaveat: A pain in the ass to clean. Every. Single. Time.

thread.title: My mom trots out this 1950s Mirro “Cooky Press” every holiday season to make cheese straws. It belonged to my grandmother. Still works great after almost 70 years!
comments: It’s built so well the company went out of business. My mom makes spritz cookies with it and every 5 years or so I have to go on eBay and buy a new one.  The way they break is the inside piece comes Off the end of the threaded piece.
Same exact one I grew up with.  No doubt it's still floating around mom's kitchen.

I have the same vintage press, except the end caps on mine are copper!  I love that people still use these.  Beware of the black coating on the handle.  It will flake off eventually (looks like it may have already started).  Once it started to flake I used a plastic scrubber and barkeepers friend to take it all off before it ended up in my cookies.
Ha! Not quite, but I like the way you think. Cheese straws are a beloved Southern US snack. Crunchy, cheesy, spicy…delicious!
I just gave mine to the goodwill, or I would offer it to you!

expected_mentions.flawOrCaveat: inside piece comes off the end of the threaded piece
black coating on the handle will flake off eventually

thread.title: Stumbled upon this old Tappan oven in a FB group today. Thought you all would like seeing it still in use.
comments: We are still using our GE double ovens from 1963! It’s a shame things aren’t made to last like this anymore.
Genuinely haven’t a clue how this is possible. The old GE I grew up with, the heating element kept going out, and eventually it was impossible to get replacements.

This is my thought too.  Every old appliance (pre-80s) I've used or owned were difficult to find parts for or didn't match the functional quality of newer appliances.  With ovens in particular, how evenly is that thing cooking or does the owner know all of the "trucks" to use it correctly?

The double oven in my moms house recently died and its gas. It was made in 1999 so it put up a good fight. Finding a gas double oven that is 24" wide is practically impossible now so I looked on facebook marketplace and found a 1970's O'Keefe and Merritt for $20. Installed it and it works perfectly. Its pristine too.

expected_mentions.flawOrCaveat: heating element kept going out, impossible to get replacements

thread.title: Found this vintage Schott NYC Made in USA leather jacket for $10 at Goodwill. Been looking for a lifetime durable leather jacket and I think this thick guy is it. They retail for quite a bit!
comments:
Wow, great find! $10? It looks brand new. Similar ones retail for about $700, you found a smoking deal. Schott makes great stuff.

If you’re curious about the provenance you could email Gail at Schott, and she probably could tell you a lot from a picture of the jacket’s label.
My Goodwill must be run by assholes...a pair of Dickies with mustard stains or a gym tshirt from 2003 is about all you'll find for $10.

Thanks for the info! I emailed their customer service email as I don't have Gail's email. 

You sound familiar with their stuff!

The jacket has minor fading and wear. I'm having it restored and cleaned and tailored to fit as we speak. 
I'm trying to find ways to maintain the jacket after.
You lucky mother fucker. You fuckin lucky mother fucker you

thread.title: My dad has had these Vasque boots for 30 years, and he backpacked across Europe in them.
comments: Beautiful leather, especially on top!

I believe that's because it may be a type of leather similar to shell-Cordovan. The obvious age of these beauties also comes from those nice full-grain uppers. 

(Peep dat hardware too)

thread.title: I think I have a bit of a boot infatuation.
comments:
Thought BIFL might appreciate these!




Left to right: 

* Alden Indy 405 (USA) - (1 year old)

* Red Wing Iron Rangers (USA) - (10 months old)

* Limmer Mid-Weight (Germany) - (3 months old - still breaking them in)

* L.L. Bean 10" Gore-tex/thinsulate (USA) - (1 year old)

* Red Wing 2414 (USA) - (6 months old)




I'm a geologist (environmental consultant) who does a fair amount of field work, so I have office/casual wear, hiking boots, rain/winter boots, and work boots. I would suggest any of these boots to any of you for their given purposes. All are made with great materials and were purchased with BIFL in mind.
[deleted]

Presentation is important! Boots are only clean because they are all fairly new.


Edit: and 5 pairs? Look at their purposes. I don't mean to seem like a dick, but these all have a different purpose. It's not like I have 5 pairs of Alden dress boots, all in different leather types and colors.  I made the post because there was another boot post on BIFL today and I though I'd throw my collection of BIFL footwear into the mix, since I spent some time reviewing and considering each before buying them.

thread.title: Hamilton watch from 1987, changed the battery and it still works
comments: Just a heads up- the time is wrong.  Should be 12:35.

*We can end this war at Yorktown, cut them off at sea, but*

*For this to succeed, there is someone else we need...*
Huh mine says 2:06

thread.title: My Neighbor’s 1985 GE Microwave
thread.body: Still in great condition and per my neighbor, has never required any repairs or maintenance. I was born the same year this microwave was made. Pretty wild to see a 40 year old microwave still working!
comments: The orange lettering brings back some childhood memories for me.

We got to install microwave ovens, custom kitchen deliveries

We got to move these refrigerators, we got to move these color TVs
It looks more futuristic to me than many new microwaves. Seems like they really stylized it, but the new microwaves (at least the cheap ones) don't seem to have much flare to them.

thread.title: Someone left a 2000’s Kenmore Canister vacuum out with the trash, only had a collapsed/clogged tube that I easily fixed! Got some extra goodies to bring it up to snuff too!
comments: I have one similar to this that was handed down to me. Initially it made nasty air...

This is a Kenmore Canister Model: 116.23613300 which users on r/Sears told me was from 2003!

I ended up buying a new Exhaust filter, Chamber filter, and a 6-pack of bags for it!
I'm almost more impressed that you found not one but three Sears stores that were still open
Yep, and they are all in the same mall!
FIITTFL?

I worked at Sears in 2004-2005 when I was in highschool selling vacuums. These were the top sellers there's was 3 different models of canister Kenmores... The Dyson's were just starting to pickup in popularity.

Vacuum cleaners in the trash are almost always just clogged. 

Our church actually wears them out...
My family has been using this vacuum since the 2000s and it works great! Used it today actually. Good find!
I had a model very similar to this and the plastic shell (primarily on the top of the carpet sweeper) just started crumbling a year or two ago.
The plastic shell on my Kenmore is in ok condition...
This vacuum arrived at the perfect time because a Shop-Vac we used to clean our cars broke finally broke (rip).
Don’t forget to replace the filters!
No problem! It’s always the old stuff that lasts forever
Maybe “find it in the trash for life”
Ah, got it.
Correct!
That thing is dope!
Yes, the accessory cover was sticky--an imperfect design
I have a very similar one, they are great!
The sucking power is what shocked me the most, this thing *GOES*
Yea, it sucks.

expected_mentions.flawOrCaveat: collapsed/clogged tube that was easily fixed

thread.title: Casio G-Shock watches can be considered BuyItForLife?
comments: mine have always died around the 8-9 year mark....
My dad has one that he’s had since the 80s. Still works....

I have one that I've worn several 1000's of days. It has only ever needed band pin replacement. It never needs adjustment, and it charges in indoor light. No battery replacement, ever. I would classify it as BIFL.
I’ve had the Rangeman for 13 years. Never taken it off and I’ve beat the absolute fuck out of it. So far I’ve had to replace the bands 3 times and the bezel cover once
...if you are truly looking for a bifl g-shock, you should go with a metal one. Resin cases and straps of g-shocks will inevitably get brittle and crack with use and time

expected_mentions.flawOrCaveat: had to replace the bands 3 times and the bezel cover once
Resin cases and straps will inevitably get brittle and crack with use and time

thread.title: Looking for a quality computer chair :-)
comments: Have a look DXRacer or AKRacing. Similar styles, well made and will last basically forever. If you have the opportunity to go to a physical store, try sitting in a few and find the model that fits your frame.
Thanks for the recommendation. How long have you had yours?
While not a real bifl, ikea - markus is a pretty good deal, in my country it comes with a ten year warranty and if it has that warranty where you live I suggest getting it :3
High quality office chairs are available refurbished, usually advertised on Craigslist. I got a $1200 Herman Miller for $300. This is a good place to spend, you'll thank yourself every time you sit.

Still salty about the Markus. That warranty is only valid if you are the original owner. So a secondhand claim isn’t valid. Go for it if you buy in store, but caution otherwise. 

Mine broke within 2 years. I even tried to pay for repairs, or at least the replacement parts needed to fix, and IKEA wouldn’t have it.
I bolted a car seat onto a pc chair base. I love it - it even tilts still.
I'll look into it. Thanks for looking out!
I feel like Craigslist is more of a gamble...but I also have zero Craigslist shopping experience to back up my suspicions...
Props for the DIY method but I don't have car seats laying around lol
I second this. The Herman Miller Aeron chair is possibly the single most comfortable chair I’ve sat in with this type of use case.
I myself have an AKRacing chair for \~4 years now. It still holds up well, nothing broken or anything, everything works fine. I don't regret buying it.
Not sure about being BIFL yet but the hyken chair at Staples often goes on sale and when it does, it's hard to get a better quality for dollar imo.
My wife and I both have SteelCase Leap V2 chairs and love them.

I had a Herman Miller chair, not an aeron but still fully adjustable, and it lasted me for 15 years and now my dad has it for his office and is still using it.  Daily use.  So I'm pretty happy with their chairs in general, while they can be costly, they're definitely worth it.

I had a Markus for about 7 years till one of the armrest bolts sheered off.   Replaced it with an [ErgoChair2](https://www.autonomous.ai/office-chairs/ergonomic-chair) which is going on 2 years now.   Im happy with it so far, its sturdy and comfortable and has great adjustments.

thread.title: Most of my Hifi components are getting on for 50 years old in the next few years. All still working perfectly
comments:
The newest components are 

1982 Sansui XR-Q7 Turntable
1982 Nakamichi BX-125 Cassette deck
1985 Braun Atelier CD player 
1992 Sony TC-K990ES
1997 Teac MD500i Mini Disc

Everything else in the setup is mid 1970s lol, best era for design and sound quality imo
Speakers?
Shoutout from the minidisc gang!! Beautiful setup and very well put together. I’m jealous.

My partners dad died over 20 years ago & left behind an easy £100k+ of Hi-Fi, he was born in 1933 & it was his lifetimes collection - he had a whole wall of actively used Hifi with interconnects & switching apparatus / devices & enough Hifi left over for approx another 2 walls. 

He had a massive Revox Amp, approx 20 Teac, 7 Revox & 30 other brand reel to reels. 

& (from what I remember) 

A Nakamichi Dragon Cassete player

Tannoy Westminster Speakers
& although he hated CDs he strangely had a Linn CD Player with DAC Converter., ****

Most of it was scattered throuout her family when he passed - (she has 12 siblings). 
He probably had about £1000+ in just cabling etc (mostly Cambridge Audio). 

 Well over 1000 reels of tape & vinyl LPs. 

Its a shame it was never sold to another collector as a whole as lots of it was damaged & thrown out over the last 2 decades, which I often think was tragic. 


She still has the Marantz & Vinyls though.
Techmoan
